stage_1_user_closing.description: Final user-turn instruction appended after alphabet and OCR blocks, before page images. Shared by benchmark and inference Stage 1 runs.
stage_1_user_closing.prompt: Now transcribe every line of text from the dictionary page image exactly as it appears. Preserve all diacritics and special characters.

stage_1_system_benchmark.description: Stage 1 system prompt for flat-mode OCR in benchmark mode (--benchmark). One ordered lines list per page (header, lines, footer). Used when comparing model output to human gold transcripts on the evaluation dataset.
stage_1_system_benchmark.prompt: You are a precise OCR transcription system specialising in historical and minority-language dictionaries. Your task is faithful OCR only — do NOT parse dictionary entries or assign fields. Output structure: - `header`: page-level lines at the very top (running title, page number, letter band). One string per visible line. Empty list if none. Never put dictionary entries here. - `lines`: every visible BODY line in reading order. For multi-column pages, transcribe the full left column top-to-bottom, then the next column, and so on — as a single ordered list (no column_id labels). - `footer`: page-level lines at the very bottom (page numbers, footnotes, rules). One string per visible line. Empty list if none. You may receive ... from a standard OCR engine. Use it only for ambiguous character shapes; always prioritise the page image. Rules for every line in header, lines, and footer: - Preserve ALL diacritics, stress marks, and special phonetic symbols exactly. - Wrap bold text in ... and italic text in ... when confident. - Do NOT interpret, summarise, merge lines, or fix typos. - Do NOT skip lines, including continuations and cross-references. - Hyphenated wraps: when a word breaks across two printed lines with a trailing hyphen, emit TWO separate strings (e.g. "intelligi-" then "ble, adj. clear").

stage_1_system_inference.description: Stage 1 system prompt for flat-mode OCR in production inference (mudidi run). Same pure-OCR instructions as benchmark; current-page labeling is in the user turn.
stage_1_system_inference.prompt: You are a precise OCR transcription system specialising in historical and minority-language dictionaries. Your task is faithful OCR only — do NOT parse dictionary entries or assign fields. Output structure: - `header`: page-level lines at the very top (running title, page number, letter band). One string per visible line. Empty list if none. Never put dictionary entries here. - `lines`: every visible BODY line in reading order. For multi-column pages, transcribe the full left column top-to-bottom, then the next column, and so on — as a single ordered list (no column_id labels). - `footer`: page-level lines at the very bottom (page numbers, footnotes, rules). One string per visible line. Empty list if none. You may receive ... from a standard OCR engine. Use it only for ambiguous character shapes; always prioritise the page image. Rules for every line in header, lines, and footer: - Preserve ALL diacritics, stress marks, and special phonetic symbols exactly. - Wrap bold text in ... and italic text in ... when confident. - Do NOT interpret, summarise, merge lines, or fix typos. - Do NOT skip lines, including continuations and cross-references. - Hyphenated wraps: when a word breaks across two printed lines with a trailing hyphen, emit TWO separate strings (e.g. "intelligi-" then "ble, adj. clear").

page_boundary_rules.description: Cross-page entry ownership for Stage 2 inference. Assigns \lx blocks to the page where the headword starts; overflow sub-fields attach to that page.
page_boundary_rules.prompt:
Page boundary rules (entry ownership):
- Assign each main dictionary entry (\lx headword block) to the page where that headword first appears.
- INCLUDE in the current page MDF:
  • Every entry whose \lx starts on this page — include ALL sub-fields (\se, \va, \sn, examples, inflection lists) even when those sub-fields print at the top of the next page. Copy characters verbatim from the <next_page> transcript and image into the parent entry on THIS page.
  • Example: if \lx ala starts on page_53 and sub-forms like —alas nuwebi print at the top of page_54, page_53's MDF must include those sub-entries; page_54 must not emit them as orphan blocks.
- EXCLUDE from the current page MDF:
  • Lines at the top that continue an entry whose \lx started on the previous page (check <previous_page> transcript and image). Do not emit them as standalone \se/\ge records or new \lx entries.
  • Em-dash sub-forms (e.g. —alas nuwebi) under a parent headword from the previous page belong in the previous page's entry block, not here.
- Neighbor page images arrive before the CURRENT page image. Use them for layout and for cross-page entry completion as described above.

stage_1_column_system.description: Legacy Stage 1 system prompt for column-mode OCR (--stage1-mode column). Outputs structured JSON with per-column column_id lines. Used for benchmark evaluation and silver-standard pre-annotation (e.g. Label Studio column TSV gold), not the default for new dictionary inference.
stage_1_column_system.prompt: You are a precise OCR transcription system specialising in historical and minority-language dictionaries. Step 1 — Detect page-level header and footer (if present). Many dictionary pages have a running title, page number, chapter abbreviation, or alphabetic letter band at the very top, and/or page numbers, footnotes, or decorative rules at the very bottom. These are NOT dictionary entries. - Top metadata → emit one string per visible line into the `header` list. - Bottom metadata → emit one string per visible line into the `footer` list. - If a region has no metadata, leave its list empty. Headers and footers can sit ANYWHERE horizontally (centred, spanning columns, or aligned to one side). Do NOT force them into a column — always treat them as page-level. The first dictionary entry of the page belongs in a column, not in `header`. Step 2 — Detect body columns. After excluding header/footer regions, examine the body layout. Most dictionary pages use one or two columns; some use three. Identify each column region (left, center, right) before transcribing. Step 3 — Transcribe each column separately, left to right. For every column, list every visible line top to bottom, exactly as it appears. Never mix a line from one column into another column's list. Do NOT include header or footer text inside any column. You may or may not given a ocr reference text wrap around by ..., it comes from a standard OCR result of the page. This text is a reference for the character shapes and should be used to help you transcribe the page. However, prioritise the visual image over OCR text — standard OCR may miss or misinterpret certain phonetic characters. Rules that apply to every line (header, footer, and column lines alike): - Preserve ALL diacritics, stress marks, and special phonetic symbols exactly. - Preserve visual formatting: wrap bold text in ... and italic text in .... Only mark formatting you are confident about — when in doubt, leave text plain. These tags may appear anywhere within a line (e.g. "headword translation"). - Do NOT interpret, restructure, summarise, or reorder content. - Do NOT skip any line, even if it looks like a sub-entry, continuation, or cross-reference. - Do NOT correct apparent typos or inconsistencies. - For single-column pages, output one column with column_id='single'. - Hyphenated line breaks: when a word is split across two physical lines with a trailing hyphen (typesetting wrap), emit the two parts as TWO SEPARATE lines exactly as printed, INCLUDING the trailing hyphen. NEVER join them. This is one of the most common inconsistencies; be strict about it. Example — the page shows: intelligi- ble, adj. clear, comprehensible. You MUST emit two lines: "intelligi-" "ble, adj. clear, comprehensible." NOT one merged line "intelligible, adj. clear, comprehensible." and NOT a single line "intelligi-ble, adj. ...". Stage 2 will rejoin hyphenated words when it forms entry text — your job is faithful copy only.

stage_1_user_alphabet.description: User-turn block appended when an alphabet file is provided. Gives the model script-specific character and diacritic guidance for the source language.
stage_1_user_alphabet.prompt:
<alphabet>
{alphabet_text}
</alphabet>

The <alphabet> is a reference guide to the list of characters in the script of the source language, not a strict whitelist. It may be
incomplete or not perfectly match this document's script variant.

Rules:
1. Prefer <alphabet> matches over visually similar characters from other scripts.
2. For combinatorial scripts (Indic conjuncts, Ethiopic syllables, Hangul
blocks, Arabic ligatures, pointed Hebrew/Syriac), treat <alphabet>
entries as base components and form composites using the script's
standard rules (virama, vowel diacritics, contextual forms).
3. If a glyph is clearly a legitimate character of the target script but
not in <alphabet> (archaic letters, extensions, related-language
characters), transcribe it correctly anyway — do not force-fit.
4. Preserve diacritics, tone marks, case, and period-specific orthography
exactly as shown.
5. Mark truly unidentifiable glyphs as [?].

variables.name: alphabet_text
variables.description: Alphabet or character legend for the source-language script; inserted between <alphabet> tags.

stage_1_user_ocr_reference.description: User-turn block appended when OCR hint text is available (e.g. Mathpix). Character-shape reference only; the page image always takes precedence.
stage_1_user_ocr_reference.prompt:
<ocr_reference>
{ocr_hint}
</ocr_reference>

The OCR reference above may contain errors but can help you identify ambiguous character shapes.

variables.name: ocr_hint
variables.description: Optional OCR reference text from Mathpix or another engine; wrapped in <ocr_reference> tags for character-shape hints.

mdf_marker_reference.description: Reference vocabulary of SIL Toolbox MDF markers. Injected into Stage 2 Pass 1 system prompt and into Pass 2 when the Toolbox manual is attached as text instead of PDF images.
mdf_marker_reference.prompt:
MDF marker vocabulary (SIL Toolbox — assign ONLY markers whose content appears on
THIS dictionary's intro + sample page; pick gloss/usage/definition tier to match target
languages: e=English, n=national, r=regional, v=vernacular):

── Entry structure (section boundaries; \se, \ps, \sn order affects MDF printing) ──
  lx   Lexeme / headword. Record marker; one per lexical entry; primary sort key.
       Use \lc instead when a more readable citation form should print.
  hm   Homonym number. Marks unrelated homographs; digits 1, 2, 3 immediately after \lx
       (no punctuation). Cross-refs may include homonym, e.g. \cf lemma2.
  lc   Lexical citation. Substitute printed headword when \lx alone is not reader-friendly
       (e.g. root form in database, inflected form in print). Replaces \lx in output.
  se   Subentry. Like \lx but inside the record — polymorphemic form or phrase derived
       from the main lexeme; may have its own senses, glosses, and examples.
  sn   Sense number. Marks start of each sense when one entry has multiple meanings;
       use 1, 2, 3 (no punctuation). Include \sn 1 when multiple senses exist.
  mn   Main entry form. Points a minimal/minor entry back to its full main entry
       ("See main entry: …").

── Glosses (short translation; used for interlinearizing and as gloss when no definition) ──
  ge   Gloss (English). Short English equivalent; prints if no \de; used for reversal
       if no \re. Multiple glosses: separate fields or one field with " ; " separator.
  gn   Gloss (national). Short national-language equivalent; prints if no \dn; used for
       reversal if no \rn. Same semicolon / multi-field conventions as \ge.
  gr   Gloss (regional). Clarifies national gloss in a regional language; prints if no \dr.
  gv   Gloss (vernacular). Vernacular gloss, mainly monolingual lexicons.

── Definitions (longer explication of semantic domain; suppresses gloss in print) ──
  de   Definition (English). Fully expresses meaning in English; if present, \ge is omitted
       from formatted dictionary output.
  dn   Definition (national). Full national-language definition of each sense.
  dr   Definition (regional). Regional-language definition (often early project data).
  dv   Definition (vernacular). Vernacular definition, mainly monolingual lexicons.
  lt   Literal meaning. Elucidates parts of an idiom or complex phrase ("Lit: …").

── Reversal / finderlist (normally does NOT print in the dictionary body) ──
  re   Reversal form (English). English index form for reversing the lexicon; \re * excludes
       entry from reversed index. Multiple forms: separate \re lines or " ; " in one field.
  rn   Reversal form (national). National-language index form; same conventions as \re.
  rr   Reversal form (regional). Regional reversal form; rarely used in MDF output.

── Word-level glosses (morpheme-level interlinear, not full-sentence translation) ──
  we   Word gloss (English).
  wn   Word gloss (national).
  wr   Word gloss (regional).

── Grammar & inflectional form ──
  ps   Part of speech. Classifies the vernacular lexeme (not the translation language).
       Use Toolbox range sets for consistent labels. Must precede \pn if both present.
  pn   Part of speech (national). National-language POS label; follows \ps; replaces \ps
       in output when formatting for a national audience.
  ph   Phonetic / phonemic form. Pronunciation when orthography hides phonetic detail;
       typically printed in square brackets.
  pl   Plural form. Vernacular plural of the lexeme ("Pl: …" in output).
  sg   Singular form. Vernacular singular noun form.
  pd   Paradigm set. Inflectional paradigm table for the lexeme.
  rd   Reduplication form(s). Reduplicated variant for later analysis ("Redup: …").
  mr   Morphemic representation. Underlying morpheme breakdown ("Morph: …").

── Examples (bundled: \rf + \xv + \xe/\xn/\xr per example) ──
  xv   Example sentence (vernacular). Short, natural usage illustrating the sense; crucial
       for verbs and sense disambiguation. Starts an example bundle if \rf is absent.
  xe   Example translation (English). Free translation of \xv.
  xn   Example translation (national). Free translation of \xv.
  xr   Example translation (regional). Free translation of \xv.
  xg   Interlinear gloss line. Morpheme-aligned gloss tier for interlinearizing.
  rf   Reference to source. Notebook/text reference for the following example sentence.

── Semantics & lexical relations ──
  sd   Semantic domain. English catalog label for semantic compartment (Louw-Nida style);
       does not normally print.
  is   Index of semantics. Semantic-domain index for related entries; does not print.
  th   Thesaurus. Vernacular-based thesaurus domain label; does not normally print.
  sy   Synonym. Simple synonym link (prefer \lf + \lv + \le/\ln for glossed relations).
  an   Antonym. Simple antonym link.
  lf   Lexical function label. Relationship code (e.g. Syn, Ant, Gen) in a lexical network.
  lv   Vernacular lexeme referenced by \lf.
  le   Gloss of \lv (English).
  ln   Gloss of \lv (national).
  lr   Gloss of \lv (regional).
  cf   Cross-reference. Links related entries (\cf target + optional \ce/\cn/\cr gloss).
  ce   Cross-reference gloss (English). Bundled with \cf.
  cn   Cross-reference gloss (national). Bundled with \cf.
  cr   Cross-reference gloss (regional). Bundled with \cf.

── Etymology & encyclopedic ──
  et   Etymology. Proto-form or source form (include leading * if desired in print).
  eg   Etymology gloss. Gloss of the etymology form.
  es   Etymology source. Source reference; normally hidden in print.
  ec   Etymology comment. Analyst comment on etymology; normally hidden in print.
  ee   Encyclopedic information (English).
  en   Encyclopedic information (national).
  er   Encyclopedic information (regional).
  ev   Encyclopedic information (vernacular). Mainly monolingual lexicons.
  bw   Borrowed word. Loan-source form.

── Variants, usage, restrictions ──
  va   Variant forms. Dialect or orthographic variant of \lx or \se; heads \ve/\vn/\vr
       comment bundle ("Variant: …").
  ve   Variant comment (English).
  vn   Variant comment (national).
  vr   Variant comment (regional).
  ue   Usage (English). Common usage, register, or restrictions so non-native speakers use
       the lexeme correctly ("Usage: …").
  un   Usage (national). National-language usage or restriction note; closely related to
       \dn/\on but for usage context, not full definitions.
  ur   Usage (regional). Regional usage note.
  uv   Usage (vernacular). Vernacular usage note; mainly monolingual lexicons.
  oe   Restriction / "only" (English). Limits when or how the lexeme applies ("Restrict:").
  on   Restriction (national).
  or   Restriction (regional).
  ov   Restriction (vernacular).

── Other (uncommon on printed body pages) ──
  sc   Scientific name. Latin biological name; MDF italicizes/underlines automatically.
  so   Source of data. Informant name/village; normally does not print.
  st   Status. Editorial completeness flag (e.g. OK, check); normally does not print.
  dt   Datestamp. Last-edited date; normally does not print.
  bb   Bibliographical reference. Further reading pointer.
  pc   Picture / graphic link.
  tb   Table. Preformatted tabular text (line breaks and tabs preserved).
  na, nd, ng, np, nq, ns, nt   Compiler notes by domain (anthropology, discourse, grammar,
       phonology, questions, sociolinguistics, general); bracketed if printed.

stage_2_pass_1.description: Stage 2 Pass 1 system prompt (shared by benchmark and inference). Instructs the model to discover which MDF markers this dictionary uses and how entries are structured.
stage_2_pass_1.prompt:
You analyse dictionary front matter and a sample page to list which SIL Toolbox MDF
markers this dictionary uses and how entries are structured.

Output a single JSON object — NOT a full typography map. For each marker that appears
in this dictionary, give a one-line description (role + language tier). Add structure
rules for entry boundaries (homographs, senses, subentries, gloss lines).

Use MDF marker codes from the reference below. Pick gloss/usage/definition tiers to
match the dictionary's target languages (e=English, n=national, r=regional, v=vernacular).
Do NOT assign English-tier markers (ge, de, ue) when the dictionary has no English target.

{mdf_marker_reference}

variables.name: mdf_marker_reference
variables.description: Full SIL Toolbox MDF marker vocabulary reference injected from the mdf_marker_reference prompt.

stage_2_pass_2.description: Stage 2 Pass 1 user prompt when one sample page is selected (--parse-rules-page). Produces parse-rules.json from introduction images plus one sample page transcription.

variables.name: transcription
variables.description: Flat Stage 1 transcription of the sample dictionary page.

variables.name: config_hint
variables.description: Optional language-role hint (layout, source, targets) from dictionary_languages config.

stage_2_pass_2_multi.description: Stage 2 Pass 1 user prompt when two or more sample pages are provided. Merges marker and layout conventions from heterogeneous sections into one parse-rules.json.

variables.name: config_hint
variables.description: Optional language-role hint (layout, source, targets) from dictionary_languages config.

variables.name: sample_pages_block
variables.description: One <sample_transcription page="STEM">…</sample_transcription> block per sample page.

stage_2_toolbox_pdf_section.description: User-turn label line when the Toolbox MDF Reference Manual PDF is attached as images during Stage 2 Pass 2 (models that accept PDF vision input).
stage_2_toolbox_pdf_section.prompt: 4. Attached SIL Toolbox MDF Reference Manual (PDF) — marker reference.

stage_2_toolbox_text_section.description: User-turn Toolbox excerpt as inline text when the model cannot ingest PDF images directly. Injects mdf_marker_reference content instead of attaching the manual PDF.
stage_2_toolbox_text_section.prompt:
4. SIL Toolbox MDF Reference Manual (text excerpt) — marker reference:

{mdf_marker_reference}

variables.name: mdf_marker_reference
variables.description: MDF marker vocabulary text when the Toolbox manual is attached as text instead of PDF images.

stage_2_direct_mdf_system_benchmark.description: Stage 2 Pass 2 system prompt in benchmark mode. Assumes human gold Stage 1 transcript; used for MDF evaluation against gold labels.
stage_2_direct_mdf_system_benchmark.prompt:
You digitize dictionary pages into SIL Toolbox MDF (Multi-Dictionary Formatter) text.

The flat transcription is human gold-label OCR text. Copy every vernacular and gloss
character from it exactly — do NOT re-read, correct, normalise, or substitute letters
from the page image. Use the page image and field map (from Pass 1) only to decide entry boundaries,
field roles, and MDF marker assignment.

Output ONLY MDF text — no JSON, no markdown fences, no commentary.
Use one blank line between lexicon records (between main entry blocks).

Allowed changes (structure only — never alter alphabet/characters):
  - Strip typography markup (<b>, <i>) when emitting MDF lines; do not change the
    underlying characters inside those spans.
  - Rejoin hyphenated line breaks from the transcription when forming headwords/glosses.
  - Normalise sense/homograph numbers: \sn and \hm as integers 1, 2, 3, …
    (convert Roman numerals; strip trailing ')' or '.').
  - Strip end-of-sentence punctuation (., !, ?) from gloss, definition, and example
    field values — MDF does not require them.
  - Split or merge lines across MDF marker fields per the field map (e.g. \un vs \gn).
  - For note fields (\np, \nt, \ng, etc.): when the field map or transcription includes a
    printed English label (e.g. "Tone:", "Commonly used classifier:"), keep that label in the
    field value unless the field map explicitly says otherwise.

Do NOT invent entries or text spans absent from the transcription.

stage_2_direct_mdf_system_inference.description: Stage 2 Pass 2 system prompt in production inference. Uses predicted Stage 1 transcript and includes editable page-boundary rules.
stage_2_direct_mdf_system_inference.prompt:
You digitize dictionary pages into SIL Toolbox MDF (Multi-Dictionary Formatter) text.

The flat transcription is the Stage-1 OCR text for this page. Copy every vernacular and gloss
character from it exactly — do NOT re-read, correct, normalise, or substitute letters
from the page image. Use the page image and field map (from Pass 1) only to decide entry boundaries,
field roles, and MDF marker assignment.

Output ONLY MDF text — no JSON, no markdown fences, no commentary.
Use one blank line between lexicon records (between main entry blocks).

Allowed changes (structure only — never alter alphabet/characters):
  - Strip typography markup (<b>, <i>) when emitting MDF lines; do not change the
    underlying characters inside those spans.
  - Rejoin hyphenated line breaks from the transcription when forming headwords/glosses.
  - Normalise sense/homograph numbers: \sn and \hm as integers 1, 2, 3, …
    (convert Roman numerals; strip trailing ')' or '.').
  - Strip end-of-sentence punctuation (., !, ?) from gloss, definition, and example
    field values — MDF does not require them.
  - Split or merge lines across MDF marker fields per the field map (e.g. \un vs \gn).
  - For note fields (\np, \nt, \ng, etc.): when the field map or transcription includes a
    printed English label (e.g. "Tone:", "Commonly used classifier:"), keep that label in the
    field value unless the field map explicitly says otherwise.

Do NOT invent entries or text spans absent from the current-page transcription or from neighbor transcripts when completing cross-page entry blocks owned by this page (see page boundary rules).

{page_boundary_rules}

variables.name: page_boundary_rules
variables.description: Injected from the page_boundary_rules prompt (inference mode only).

stage_2_direct_mdf_user_benchmark.description: Stage 2 Pass 2 user prompt in benchmark mode. Wraps gold transcript, parse-rules field block, and optional toolbox section.
stage_2_direct_mdf_user_benchmark.prompt: Dictionary page extraction. Inputs: 1. Gold-label OCR transcription (bold = …, italic = …) or column TSV (column_id, line_number, text). Treat this as authoritative for all characters. 2. Photo of the dictionary page (image below) — layout and field-boundary reference only. {toolbox_section} {field_block} Parse every dictionary entry on the page into MDF text using the field map above. Copy headword and gloss characters verbatim from the transcription except for the structural normalisations listed in the system prompt. {transcription} {guides_block}

variables.name: transcription
variables.description: Gold-label or predicted Stage 1 transcript for the page; authoritative for vernacular and gloss characters.

variables.name: field_block
variables.description: Formatted parse rules block from Pass 1 (parse-rules.json).

variables.name: guides_block
variables.description: Optional user-defined guidelines appended to the user turn.

variables.name: toolbox_section
variables.description: Toolbox MDF manual section (PDF attachment note or text excerpt).

stage_2_direct_mdf_user_inference.description: Stage 2 Pass 2 user prompt in production inference. Includes neighbor-page context, page-boundary rules, Stage 1 predictions, and parse-rules field block.

variables.name: transcription
variables.description: Gold-label or predicted Stage 1 transcript for the page; authoritative for vernacular and gloss characters.

variables.name: field_block
variables.description: Formatted parse rules block from Pass 1 (parse-rules.json).

variables.name: guides_block
variables.description: Optional user-defined guidelines appended to the user turn.

variables.name: toolbox_section
variables.description: Toolbox MDF manual section (PDF attachment note or text excerpt).

variables.name: previous_page_context
variables.description: Optional previous page context (image sent separately when available).

variables.name: next_page_context
variables.description: Optional next page context (image sent separately when available).

variables.name: page_boundary_rules
variables.description: Injected from the page_boundary_rules prompt (inference mode only).

variables.name: current_page_context
variables.description: Stem of the page being converted to MDF.

variables.name: page_image_order
variables.description: Ordered list of which page image is previous, next, and current.

stage_1_neighbor_context.description: Stage 1 inference only. Optional prev/next page images may help with characters or hyphenation at the margin — not for skipping visible lines.
stage_1_neighbor_context.prompt:
Optional previous/next page images may help resolve ambiguous characters or hyphenation at the top or bottom margin.
Transcribe every line visible on the CURRENT page image. Do not skip lines because they look like continuations from another page — Stage 2 handles entry boundaries later.
